think_tank_entries.title: Sri Lanka needs a development plan, not just a recovery narrative
think_tank_entries.summary:
Sri Lanka's trajectory is marked by volatility rather than steady progress. Gains in political rights, economic openness, and security proved episodic rather than transformative when not supported by a credible rule of law. Breaking from cycles of recovery and crisis requires institutional renewal, social reconciliation, and professional governance.

The post Sri Lanka needs a development plan, not just a recovery narrative appeared first on Atlantic Council.

think_tank_entries.title: Trump is fighting two wars with Iran. He needs to win both.
think_tank_entries.summary:
Nothing could be more short-sighted than leaving a vengeful and still formidable Iranian regime in place.

The post Trump is fighting two wars with Iran. He needs to win both. appeared first on Atlantic Council.

think_tank_entries.title: The Iran conflict exposes the new cost curve of war
think_tank_entries.summary:
The Pentagon must rapidly field innovative, lower-cost technologies alongside its exquisite capabilities, ensuring each is used where it delivers the greatest advantage.

The post The Iran conflict exposes the new cost curve of war appeared first on Atlantic Council.

think_tank_entries.title: What Romania’s role in the Iran war reveals about its diplomatic positioning
think_tank_entries.summary:
Amid transatlantic differences over the Iran war, Romania’s ability to maintain a balance between Washington and its European partners is a valuable strategic contribution to NATO.

The post What Romania’s role in the Iran war reveals about its diplomatic positioning appeared first on Atlantic Council.

think_tank_entries.title: The Strait of Hormuz crisis will ripple across plastics and food supply chains, helping Beijing and Moscow, hurting Americans
think_tank_entries.summary:
The oil and gas blockade in the Strait of Hormuz will tighten petrochemical and fertilizer markets, with geopolitical and economic implications for the United States.

The post The Strait of Hormuz crisis will ripple across plastics and food supply chains, helping Beijing and Moscow, hurting Americans appeared first on Atlantic Council.

think_tank_entries.title: What the Iran war means for Europe’s energy
think_tank_entries.summary: Mark Leonard is joined by Szymon Kardaś to discuss how the war in Iran is affecting energy prices and supplies in Europe

think_tank_entries.title: Eastern Europe’s breaking point: When crises collide and the EU hesitates
think_tank_entries.summary: Jessica Hendrick is joined by Sophie Gueudet and Ivanna Klympush-Tsintsadze to explore how geopolitical shocks are reshaping eastern Europe’s future

think_tank_entries.title: Fast energy: How Europe can power the AI revolution and stay competitive
think_tank_entries.summary: Europeans must urgently implement a “fast energy” programme to speed up access to energy to power AI—and remain competitive in the world.

think_tank_entries.title: The art of the off-ramp: How Europe can pressure Trump to end the war in Iran
think_tank_entries.summary: As Trump digs his heels into a war with Iran, he faces a growing list of domestic pressures. Europeans can press on these points to push Washington towards a ceasefire

think_tank_entries.title: Beijing hold’em: European cards against Chinese coercion
think_tank_entries.summary: Europe has a China problem and pretending otherwise is making it worse. Chinese cheap exports and a monopoly on rare earths are not just economic irritants, but leverage wielded deliberately. Europeans can no longer just de-risk. They need a deterrence strategy with teeth

think_tank_entries.title: What the Iran war means for Ukraine
think_tank_entries.summary: Mark Leonard is joined by Jana Kobzova to discuss how the war in Iran is affecting Ukraine, from US support to shifting European unity

think_tank_entries.title: The Trump trap: Why Meloni’s referendum defeat is a warning for Europe
think_tank_entries.summary: The Italian prime minister’s referendum defeat could reveal the price of allying with a US administration that goes against European interests

think_tank_entries.title: Beefing up partnerships: How the EU and Australia can give their trade deal strategic heft
think_tank_entries.summary: The EU has concluded its third major trade deal of 2026. Its agreement with Australia reflects both sides’ desire for new partners amid Trumpian chaos and Chinese coercion. But now this partnership should go further

think_tank_entries.title: EV endgame: Stalling China’s export surge in Europe’s southern neighbourhood
think_tank_entries.summary: Chinese electric vehicle brands are adapting to Western trade barriers by increasing their presence in the Middle East and North Africa. Europe’s response must go beyond merely closing the loopholes

think_tank_entries.title: The Gulf on the front line: The end of strategic hedging and new space for Europeans
think_tank_entries.summary: The war on Iran marks a turning point in the security calculus of the Gulf monarchies. Strategic hedging has failed—and Europeans could become just the reliable partners the monarchies need.

think_tank_entries.title: Why China, not Russia, could be the real winner of the Iran war
think_tank_entries.summary: Russia’s oil bonanza will hardly bridge its huge fiscal gap. China’s current pains are real, but its gains could last.

think_tank_entries.title: Love Actually? Washington’s current relationship with Britain is more like Contempt Actually
think_tank_entries.summary: If the UK wants to regain serious respect in the world, it needs its European leg as well as its transatlantic one

think_tank_entries.title: Hormuz hold’em: How to stop the US-Iran crisis in the strait
think_tank_entries.summary: If Trump follows through on his threat to bomb Iran’s energy infrastructure, the Strait of Hormuz is likely to shut down entirely and the catastrophic energy crisis will deepen. Europeans need to push for an urgent and sustainable ceasefire.

think_tank_entries.title: Lebanon on the brink: Europe must act now if it wants to prevent a refugee wave
think_tank_entries.summary: With over a thousand dead and a million displaced, Lebanon is edging towards state collapse. Europe must go beyond humanitarian aid and throw its full weight behind France’s diplomatic push.

think_tank_entries.title: Hungary After the Ballot: Change, Continuity, or Contestation?
think_tank_entries.summary:
Hungary After the Ballot: Change, Continuity, or Contestation? Dimitar Keranov Fri, 04/03/2026 - 15:17
Text/Summary

Hungary’s April 12 parliamentary elections are shaping up to be the country’s most competitive and consequential since 2010, when Prime Minister Viktor Orbán returned to power. His Fidesz party faces an unprecedentedly strong challenger in Péter Magyar’s Tisza party, which has been leading the polls since the beginning of 2025. Given Fidesz’s capture of the state and the distorted electoral playing field, however, a Tisza victory remains far from certain.

Fidesz’s campaign accuses Ukraine of interfering in the race in support of Tisza, while the opposition focuses on governance failures and corruption, and the resulting socioeconomic challenges faced by the population. Emerging allegations of Russian support for the Orbán government—which has, over the years, become the Kremlin’s Trojan horse in the EU—underscore the broader geopolitical significance of the elections.

A Tisza victory could open the way for a difficult but potentially transformative reset domestically and in Hungary’s relationship with the EU. A Fidesz victory, in contrast, would likely push the country further down an authoritarian path and entrench its obstructionist stance within the bloc, risking paralysis in decision-making at a particularly vulnerable moment. The outcome of the elections will consequently shape Hungary’s and the EU's immediate future.

This panel, convening the day after the vote, will provide a first assessment of the results and their immediate political consequences. It will explore if the electoral process met democratic standards, the consequences of any irregularities, the likely composition and priorities of the next government, and the broader implications for illiberal politics and democratic resilience in Europe.

To register for the event, please click on the button above. Details for access will be sent to registrants. For more information, please contact Zsuzsanna Végh at ZVegh@gmfus.org.


The German Marshall Fund of the United States (GMF) is a nonpartisan organization that advances the transatlantic partnership to meet the challenges and opportunities of the 21st century.
 
With headquarters in Washington, DC; offices in Berlin, Brussels, Paris, Warsaw, Belgrade, Bucharest, and Ankara; representations in London and Madrid; and more than 140 experts and fellows worldwide, GMF is a hub for policy innovation, convening, and leadership development.
 
GMF’s work focuses on three priorities: strengthening transatlantic security, advancing economic and technological competitiveness, and adapting to a shifting global landscape.

think_tank_entries.title: Climate change and central banking: macroeconomic challenges and evidence from Africa
think_tank_entries.summary: Climate change is an important source of macroeconomic risk with direct implications for monetary policy and financial stability. Physical risks, including more frequent and severe weather events, disrupt production and damage infrastructure, generating supply and demand side pressures that affect output and inflation.

think_tank_entries.title: Quantum Bayesian inference: an exploration
think_tank_entries.summary:
by Jon Frost, Carlos Madeira, Yash Rastogi, Harald Uhlig
This paper introduces a framework for performing Bayesian inference using quantum computation. It presents a proof-of-concept quantum algorithm that performs posterior sampling. We provide an accessible introduction to quantum computation for economists and a practical demonstration of quantum-based posterior sampling for Bayesian estimation.

think_tank_entries.title: Labour market flows, unemployment and the Phillips curve
think_tank_entries.summary:
by Enisse Kharroubi, Marius Koechlin
We present empirical evidence from the United States demonstrating that labour market flows provide valuable insights into subsequent wage and price inflation.

think_tank_entries.title: Opening doors to open finance: evidence from the international experience
think_tank_entries.summary: Open finance is transforming the financial system, with rapid adoption in several jurisdictions in the years since its introduction. By enabling the sharing and use of customer-permissioned data, open finance can foster innovation, competition and financial inclusion. Early evidence highlights a role for open finance in breaking through data silos, reducing information asymmetries and driving venture capital investment.

think_tank_entries.title: Stablecoin flows and spillovers to FX markets
think_tank_entries.summary:
by Iñaki Aldasoro, Paula Beltrán, Federico Grinberg
Using data on four USD-pegged stablecoins and 27 fiat currencies, this paper documents spillovers from stablecoin-based foreign exchange (FX) to traditional FXmarkets. We document a gap between the cost of acquiring dollars via stablecoins and via the spot FX market (parity deviations). To establish a causal link between stablecoin flows and FX markets, we use a granular instrumental variable that exploits idiosyncratic shocks to stablecoin net inflows in other currencies.

think_tank_entries.title: Financial stability limits on fiscal space
think_tank_entries.summary:
by Fabrizio Zampolli
Conventional indicators of fiscal sustainability, such as the interest rate-growth differential that focus on long-term drivers do not always incorporate fluctuating financial conditions and risk. This paper proposes an analytical framework in which sovereign borrowing costs depend on the balance-sheet capacity of financial intermediaries, where financial amplification can generate an endogenously tighter debt limit even in the absence of fiscal fatigue or explicit default risk. Fiscal space becomes state-contingent: identical yield shocks compress fiscal space more strongly when the economy is closer to its debt limit. We examine four financial amplification mechanisms: the bank-sovereign nexus, "original sin redux" , duration matching, and deleveraging in repo markets.

think_tank_entries.title: In data we trust? Emerging policy and supervisory approaches to AI data use in financial services
think_tank_entries.summary: Data plays a critical role in the transformation of the financial sector through artificial intelligence (AI). While challenges in data management are not new, they pose significant barriers to the wider adoption of advanced AI systems, such as generative AI (gen AI). Key concerns include data privacy, quality and security, which are further intensified by third-party dependencies and market concentration among major service providers. Effectively managing data-related risks is essential to ensure that AI adoption in financial services fosters innovation while upholding trust, resilience, and financial stability.

think_tank_entries.title: Liquidity preparedness for margin and collateral calls - Executive Summary
think_tank_entries.summary: Recent episodes of market turmoil, eg the Archegos Capital collapse in 2021 or the United Kingdom's liability-driven fund stress in September 2022, showed that rapid increases in margin and collateral requirements, while essential for managing counterparty risk, can amplify liquidity needs across the financial system, especially if market participants are unprepared.

think_tank_entries.title: Lessons from the 2023 banking turmoil - Executive Summary
think_tank_entries.summary: The banking turmoil that started in March 2023 was the most significant period of banking stress since the Great Financial Crisis that began in 2007. Credit Suisse, a Swiss global systemically important bank, along with three regional banks in the United States, with total assets exceeding USD 1 trillion, were either shut down, put into receivership or rescued. These failures triggered a crisis of confidence in banks' resilience and led to wide-scale public support measures.

think_tank_entries.title: Sound management of third-party risk - Executive Summary
think_tank_entries.summary:
A third-party service provider (TPSP) is an entity or individual that performs services, activities, functions, processes or tasks directly for a bank.
Banks enter into formal arrangements with TPSPs for various reasons, including enabling access to specialised expertise, reducing costs and improving scalability, efficiency and operational resilience.

think_tank_entries.title: Supervising DEI in insurance - Executive Summary
think_tank_entries.summary: In 2021, the International Association of Insurance Supervisors (IAIS) published a statement that explained the importance of diversity, equity and inclusion (DEI) considerations in insurance supervision. Its Application Paper on supervising diversity, equity and inclusion: the governance, risk management and culture perspective elaborates on the significance of DEI in the supervision of risk management and corporate culture of insurers, in accordance with the IAIS Insurance Core Principles (ICPs).
